title: Lesson 417
transcript: Okay, so we've already talked about a whole bunch of different risk management frameworks, things like NIST 800-37, etc. Let's now talk about a few major security control frameworks that you need to know about. That's the major topic here of Section 3.3. We're going to talk through a bunch of different security control frameworks that you need to know about. So high level, what do security control frameworks provide? I mean, it goes back to who creates these frameworks? Experts. Frameworks are generally created by experts in an industry. And so if you want to think about how can you better protect your systems, often a really good way to do that is to go and look at a framework. Like if someone said, okay, you're now in charge of security at some company, a bad thing to do would probably be to take out a piece of paper and just try and make up your own entire security program. If you've never done it before, that's probably not going to go very well. A much better way of doing this would be to say, OK, well, other people have figured this. They've solved this problem. They've created security programs. Let's learn from what they did. And those experts have often compiled their collective wisdom in frameworks. So you could use something like the ISO 27001 framework or NIST 800-53. to help you figure out how to create security in your organization. So what do frameworks provide? Comprehensive guidance. They do a really good job of showing you, here's what you need to put in place to allow you to achieve your organizational goals and objectives. It's worth mentioning another major reason that security control frameworks are often of importance to an organization is you may have a contractual or regulatory requirement to be in compliance with one of these. One we're gonna talk about in just a second here is PCI, the Payment Card Industry DSS standard. If a company is processing credit cards from Visa, MasterCard, et cetera, they're gonna have to be PCI compliant. If you're in critical infrastructure, you might have to be NIRCSIP compliant. If you're in the healthcare industry, you might have to be HIPAA compliant, et cetera. So the point is these frameworks provide a really good job at sort of guiding, and they're also often a contractual or regulatory requirement to be in compliance with these things. So that's why companies care so much about security control frameworks. They're useful and they have to be in compliance. Okay, so let's start going through these frameworks. And before I start getting into any of the specifics of the frameworks, one more high-level thing here. You do not need to be an expert on any of these frameworks. You don't need to know any of them in any sort of detail. Really what you need to know is what do these different frameworks focus on? What are they particularly useful for? Because different frameworks are focused on different things and they're useful for different reasons. So you might get questions on the exam asking you, you know, where would you use COBIT versus FedRAMP or HIPAA? So what I'm going to do is just give you a high level overview, really high level of what each of these frameworks focus on. Let's start with COBIT. Do you happen to recall which organization is behind the creation of the COBIT framework? It's ISACA. And if you've heard of ISACA, ISACA is an organization made up of IT auditors, primarily IT auditors. So COBIT is a framework created by IT auditors. So what is COBIT particularly useful for? IT audit assurance work, because it's been written by IT auditors. The controls in COBIT are written in such a way that they're very easy to test. They're very easy to audit against. And so, in fact, one of the really common usages of COBIT controls is if there is some sort of, say, third-party audit being conducted where a service provider might be having their controls tested by an independent auditor, so the independent auditor could create a SOC 2 Type 2 report. We'll talk about those later. if the auditor was creating this SOC 2 Type 2 report to give to the service provider. So the service provider could then give that report out to their customers. Often the controls that are used in those audits are COBIT controls. Because they're written in such a way, you know, the access management controls, the backup controls, the network security controls. They're written in such a way that they're very easy to audit against. So that's one usage of COBIT. ITIL, if you've ever come across this framework, it does a really good job of defining all the major processes that you'd expect to have in place in a well-run security function. So basically anything with the word process in it. So your onboarding process, your access management process, your change management process, configuration management process, your backup and recovery processes. ITIL does a good job of defining all of these major processes. NIST, specifically NIST 800-53, which is the Cybersecurity Control Framework. It's a very useful framework because it does a good job of defining a broad set of security and privacy controls that an organization should have in place. And NIST is a US government entity. So NIST is primarily used by US government agencies. And a lot of US government agencies will have a requirement to show that they're in compliance with NIST 800-53, that they have the right controls in place to be compliant with it. PCI, I mentioned this in the intro, the payment card industry DSS standard. All you really need to know here, right? If a company is processing credit cards, they need to be PCI compliant. As a quick aside, have you ever had the great pleasure of being involved in a PCI audit? It's a complete nightmare of a framework. If you've never had the pleasure of being involved in a PCI audit, there's over 1,000 controls in the framework. There is zero concept of risk. So you spend an enormous amount of time testing controls that make no sense at all for a particular organization and not enough time testing controls that are important. So it's a very challenging framework to test against. I'm clearly not a big fan of it, but there you go. I mean, it's a good idea that the credit card companies have put this in place. The framework is just not super well defined or well created. All right. Enough ranting about PCI. HIPAA. The Health Information Privacy and Accountability Act, I think is what it stands for. By the way, you don't have to memorize all these acronyms for the exam. If HIPAA is ever used on the exam, or any of these acronyms are used on the exam, they'll be spelled out. So what is HIPAA used for? Well, again, this is another. In fact, all of the bottom ones here, they're all US specific frameworks. HIPAA is specific to the US. Any organization that is collecting, processing, using, that works in the healthcare industry and they're dealing with obviously sensitive patient data, they have to be HIPAA compliant. So HIPAA is a framework about protecting, safeguarding medical information. And of course, that medical information is stored on computer systems. So HIPAA contains quite a few security controls to ensure that healthcare data is appropriately protected. SOX is a really interesting one to have in this list, the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. It is in no way, shape, or form a security control framework. In fact, if you know about the origins of SOX, It came about as a direct result of the wild fraud being committed by a particular organization as part of the dot-com boom. If you were around at that time and paying attention, the company I'm talking about is Enron. There was a few other companies, but Enron was the poster child of this. So if you haven't heard about this before, Enron, in collusion with their auditors, Arthur Anderson, were committing vast amounts of fraud. And this was discovered and Enron was sued into oblivion. It was a whole mess. And what was particularly irking for investors and shareholders and so forth was that The way the laws were back then, you could sue Enron, the corporate entity, but you could not sue, you could not make the senior executives, the CEO, the CFO, the people that were actually committing the fraud, you couldn't make them personally liable because they could hide behind the corporate firewall. So you could sue Enron, which went bankrupt, but you couldn't make these individuals accountable, really. And that, frankly, pissed a lot of people off. So the U.S. Congress acted. They enacted the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. And I'm way oversimplifying this here, but... Basically, one of the provisions of SOX requires that the senior executives of publicly traded companies in the US, it requires these senior executives, like the CEO, the CFO, to sign off on the financial results that are being produced. And if it is found, that those senior executives willfully, intentionally were committing fraud. Then, and here's the key piece, they could be personally liable. And this was a tectonic shift in the way that liability worked in the US. And this obviously freaked a lot of senior executives out. They're like, wait, by signing off on this, if there's like problems in this and, you know, if I'm found out that I was committing fraud, I can be personally liable. You can take away all my millions of or billions or whatever I'm being paid. That freaked a lot of them out. And That's actually why we're talking about it here, because Sarbanes-Oxley has really helped to promote the tone from the top that we want, where we want senior executives invested in security. We want them to care about security. And Sox really helped about that because of course, senior executives realize the numbers that they have to sign off on, those numbers are coming out of computer systems. So those computer systems better have confidentiality, integrity, etc. And so SOC's not a security control framework, but it has helped to promote this tone from the top to ensure that senior executives are actually invested in having good security controls within their organization. So it's kind of an interesting one. FedRAMP. This one's easy to remember. Any US federal government agency, Fed, any US federal government agency that wants to use cloud services, those cloud services have to be FedRAMP compliant. So FedRAMP is a security control framework specific to cloud security controls. And it's actually even a whole certification framework as well attached to it. But The simple thing you have to remember for the exam is that FedRAMP is a security control framework specific to cloud security controls. And any US federal government agency that wants to use cloud services, those cloud services have to be FedRAMP compliant. And then the final one here, FISMA. This is, again, another U.S. government agency standard. And it's a requirement that certain U.S. government agencies are in compliance with FSMA. It's another security control framework. And it's actually a requirement for some U.S. government agencies. You don't need to know which ones. But it's a requirement for some U.S. government agencies to be compliant with FSMA. So it's just another security control framework. OK, so that's the ones that you need to know just a tiny bit about. There is one security control framework that you need to know a bit more about, and that is ISO 27001. And the reason you need to know more about ISO is that if you were to survey the planet asking, and trying to figure out what is the most commonly used security control framework, I think you'd find the answer is this, ISO 27001. Less so here in Canada and in the US. It's still used very commonly, but not quite as commonly. Europe, other parts of the world, it's much more pervasively used. So that's why we're gonna talk about it in a bit more detail because it is such a commonly used security control framework. So first of all, here is the official definition. ISO 27001 provides best practice recommendations for an ISMS. What the heck is an ISMS? An Information Security Management System. What is that? It's basically this, it's a security function. So what ISO 27001 provides is the best practice recommendations for a security department, a security function. So if you were to think about it, what would you expect to have in place in a well-run security function? Well, it would start with having the right governance in place. to ensure that the security function was aligned with overall corporate goals and objectives. So making sure the security function had the right policies, procedures, et cetera. You'd then, run security function, then you then want to make sure that there's a good onboarding process, a good access management process, a good asset management process, configuration and change control processes, network security controls. backup and recovery controls, cryptographic controls, a compliance function, et cetera. So if you were to step back and think about what would a well-run security function look like, that's what ISO 27001 provides. That's what an ISMS is. An information security management system is a security function. There's a companion document to ISO 27001 that you need to know about, which is ISO 27002. So here's the relationship. ISO 27001 is a much shorter document because it basically just covers the controls. ISO 27002 is a much longer document, because what ISO 27002 provides is all of the implementation guidance for the controls in 27001. Let's say you look at a control in 27001, and you're like, how would I actually implement multi-factor authentication? You could look at 27002 and you would find that there's a ton of detail in there about here's exactly how you would do that. So 27002 provides a bunch of detailed implementation guidance for how to implement the controls in 27001. And that leads to a good exam question. Which of these could an organization be certified against? And the answer is, The reason being, an auditor can come in and audit an organization against a control framework, against a set of controls. You can't be audited against a guidance document. And actually, pay attention here. This is actually very common. Again, not so much here in Canada and the US, but certainly in Europe and other parts of the world. It's very common for service providers to be ISO 27001 audited and certified. And service providers use that as a means of demonstrating that they have good security controls in place. If a customer is going to a service provider and saying, hey, Do you have good security controls? The service provider can hand them their ISO 27001 certification and say, yeah, we do. We passed. We've been ISO 27001 certified. That indicates that we have good security controls in place. So remember that. You can be certified. An organization can be certified against ISO 27001. One more little tiny tidbit here related to ISO 27001. If you were to ask the question, where in the ISO 27001 document are the controls actually listed out? The answer is in Annex A, Appendix A. So if you were to look in the ISO 27001 document, where you would find all of the, there's like, these are the 14 domains and there's about 140 odd controls. If you were to look where they actually list it out, it's in Annex A. By the way, I would not suggest you need to memorize all 14 domains here, and you certainly do not need to memorize the 140-odd controls, but... Just remember, ISO 27001, one of the most commonly used security control frameworks in the world, defines the best practice recommendations. And if you look in the control document and ask where are the controls, they're listed in an annex A. One final little thing I'm going to cover here. This is not on the exam, but I think this is just useful information to think about. If you look at all these different security control frameworks, and of course, I don't have all of them listed here. If I were to list out more, you'd see even more overlapping. The point I'm trying to show you with this little diagram here is that all of these different security control frameworks, they're not mutually exclusive. It's not like ISO 27001 just covers this stuff, and then COBIT just covers completely different controls over here. That's not at all the case. These different security control frameworks have lots of overlap between them. And the reason I mention this is that a lot of especially larger organizations, they often have to be in compliance with multiple different frameworks. And those frameworks are going to overlap. And this can create quite the compliance burden. If an organization were to sort of be audited externally or even do their internal audits against all of these, like if they were basically to audit every single framework separately, like go through all the controls that they needed to be in compliance with ISO, and then all the controls they needed to be in compliance with COVID, and all the controls they needed to be in ITIL, they'd end up retesting the same stuff over and over again. Like access management is a perfect example. All of these control frameworks cover access management. And so if you have to be in compliance with like four or five different frameworks, they're all going to look at access management controls. And so to significantly reduce the audit burden, A good practice is what's often referred to as control rationalization. This is just a fancy way of saying, look at the different control frameworks you have to be in compliance with and identify the overlapping controls. And the whole sort of Holy grail that you want to aim for here is this whole idea of test once report many. So in other words, test your access management controls just a single time. Go through, test all these different controls once, and then use the results of that testing for your ISO compliance, for your PCI compliance, for your HIPAA compliance, whatever it might be. That's the ultimate goal. I'm not going to suggest it's easy to get there. A lot of people do this as a full time job. I used to do a lot of this back when I worked at Deloitte. We did a lot of consulting around this. It's not easy to do this, but it is worth figuring out taking the time to do this because it can significantly reduce a company's compliance burden how much time they're getting spending getting tested and audited every year so just wanted to point that final little thing out there okay there we go that's security control frameworks
